---
title: "BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework"
---

```{julia}
#| include: false
include("src/bliss_rs.jl")
include("src/bliss_gp.jl")
include("src/supersonic_jet.jl")
using Printf, Plots, LaTeXStrings, Statistics, LinearAlgebra, Random, Distributions
gr()
```

## Why Quadratic Polynomials Are Not Enough

The BLISS-RS surrogate is a second-order polynomial fitted by ordinary least squares.
This choice was sensible in 2002: quadratic models are cheap to fit, analytically
differentiable, and require at most $N_S = (n^2+3n+2)/2$ evaluations. For the
supersonic business jet's aerodynamics black box ($n = 14$, $N_S = 120$) the fit is
tractable and the gradient is free.

But quadratic polynomials make a strong structural assumption: the response is smooth,
unimodal, and well-approximated by a paraboloid over the entire search interval. When
the interval is large relative to the response variation this assumption fails — the
polynomial interpolates through sample points but extrapolates poorly. Worse, it
provides no indication of *where* it is unreliable.

@fig-poly-vs-gp illustrates the difference on a one-dimensional slice through the
supersonic business jet range function.

```{julia}
#| label: fig-poly-vs-gp
#| fig-cap: "Quadratic polynomial (BLISS-RS) vs. GP (BLISS-2026) on a 1-D slice through the ssbj range function. The GP interpolates the training data exactly and provides calibrated uncertainty bands; the polynomial misses the curvature and gives no uncertainty signal."
#| code-fold: true

# 1-D slice: vary wing sweep Λ_w, hold all other vars at baseline
Z_base = [0.075, 4.0, 45.0, 400.0, 120.0, 4.5, 0.2, 45_000.0, 1.8]
lo_sw, hi_sw = 35.0, 72.0

sweep_range = range(lo_sw, hi_sw, length=200)
y_true = map(sweep_range) do Λ
    Z = copy(Z_base); Z[3] = Λ
    try ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
    catch; 0.0 end
end

# 10 training points
rng = MersenneTwister(42)
x_tr = sort(lo_sw .+ rand(rng, 10) .* (hi_sw - lo_sw))
y_tr = map(x_tr) do Λ
    Z = copy(Z_base); Z[3] = Λ
    try ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
    catch; 0.0 end
end

# Coded training / test
x_c  = (x_tr  .- lo_sw) ./ (hi_sw - lo_sw)
x_tc = (collect(sweep_range) .- lo_sw) ./ (hi_sw - lo_sw)

# Quadratic RS
X_rs = hcat(ones(10), x_c, x_c.^2)
b_rs = (X_rs'X_rs) \ (X_rs'y_tr)
X_rs_t = hcat(ones(200), x_tc, x_tc.^2)
y_rs_pred = X_rs_t * b_rs

# GP
gp_n, y_mean, y_std = fit_gp_auto(reshape(x_c,10,1), y_tr)
μ_gp, σ²_gp = predict_gp_scaled(gp_n, y_mean, y_std, reshape(x_tc, 200, 1))
σ_gp = sqrt.(max.(σ²_gp, 0.0))

p = plot(sweep_range, y_true, lw=2, color=:black, label="True range",
         xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)", ylabel="Range (NM)",
         title="Quadratic RS vs. GP Surrogate (10 training points)")
plot!(sweep_range, y_rs_pred, lw=2, color=:crimson, linestyle=:dash, label="Quadratic RS")
plot!(sweep_range, μ_gp, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="GP mean")
plot!(sweep_range, μ_gp .- 2σ_gp, fillrange=μ_gp .+ 2σ_gp,
      fillalpha=0.18, color=:steelblue, lw=0, label="GP ±2σ")
scatter!(x_tr, y_tr, color=:black, markersize=6, label="Training points")
```

Two problems are visible in @fig-poly-vs-gp:

1. The quadratic polynomial misses the local maximum near 55° and predicts
   negative range at the high-sweep extreme — physically impossible extrapolation.
2. It gives no warning that its prediction is unreliable. The GP correctly widens its
   uncertainty bands in the data-sparse high-sweep region.

## Gaussian Process Surrogates

A **Gaussian Process** is a probability distribution over functions. Any finite
collection of function values follows a multivariate normal distribution:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_1), \ldots, f(\mathbf{x}_n) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{m}, K)
$$ {#eq-gp-def}

where $m_i = m(\mathbf{x}_i)$ is the prior mean (typically zero after output
normalisation) and $K_{ij} = k(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is the **kernel
(covariance) function** encoding prior beliefs about function smoothness.

### Kernel Functions

Three kernels are implemented in `src/bliss_gp.jl`:

**Squared Exponential (SE)** — infinitely differentiable, very smooth:
$$
k_{SE}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sigma^2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}'\|^2}{2\ell^2}\right)
$$ {#eq-se-kernel}

**Matérn 5/2** — twice differentiable, preferred for physical engineering responses:
$$
k_{M52}(r) = \sigma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{\sqrt{5}\,r}{\ell} + \frac{5r^2}{3\ell^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{\sqrt{5}\,r}{\ell}\right)
$$ {#eq-matern52}

**ARD-SE** — independent length scale $\ell_i$ per dimension, allowing automatic
relevance determination: unimportant variables get large $\ell_i$ and are
effectively ignored by the surrogate.

### Posterior Inference

Given $n$ noisy observations $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{X}) + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$
with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2 I)$, the GP posterior at test points
$\mathbf{X}_*$ is:

$$
\mu(\mathbf{X}_*) = k(\mathbf{X}_*, \mathbf{X})\,(K + \sigma_n^2 I)^{-1}\,\mathbf{y}
$$ {#eq-gp-mean}

$$
\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}_*) = k(\mathbf{X}_*, \mathbf{X}_*) - k(\mathbf{X}_*, \mathbf{X})\,(K + \sigma_n^2 I)^{-1}\,k(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{X}_*)
$$ {#eq-gp-var}

In code this is computed efficiently via a Cholesky factorisation $K + \sigma_n^2 I = LL^T$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha} = L^{-T}(L^{-1}\mathbf{y}), \qquad
\mu_* = k_*^T\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \qquad
\sigma^2_* = k_{**} - \|L^{-1}k_*\|^2
$$ {#eq-cholesky-inference}

```{julia}
#| label: lst-gp-demo
#| code-fold: true

# Demonstrate GP fitting and prediction on the 1-D example
rng2 = MersenneTwister(99)
x_demo = sort(rand(rng2, 8))
y_demo = sin.(2π .* x_demo) .+ 0.1 .* randn(rng2, 8)

X_mat = reshape(x_demo, 8, 1)
gp2, ym, ys = fit_gp_auto(X_mat, y_demo)

x_test = reshape(collect(range(0,1,200)), 200, 1)
μ2, σ²2 = predict_gp_scaled(gp2, ym, ys, x_test)

@printf("Fitted SE kernel: ℓ = %.3f, σ² = %.3f\n", gp2.kernel.ℓ, gp2.kernel.σ²)
@printf("Coverage (±2σ) on training points: %.1f%%\n",
        100 * mean([abs((y_demo[i] - predict_gp(gp2, x_demo[i:i])[1][1] * ys + ym) /
                     sqrt(max(predict_gp(gp2, x_demo[i:i])[2][1], 1e-12)) / ys) < 1.96
                   for i in 1:8]))
```

### Hyperparameter Optimisation via Marginal Likelihood

The length scale $\ell$ and signal variance $\sigma^2$ are not free parameters to
be tuned by hand — they are inferred from the data itself by maximising the **log
marginal likelihood**:

$$
\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{X}, \theta) =
  -\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{y}^T K_\theta^{-1}\mathbf{y}
  -\tfrac{1}{2}\log|K_\theta|
  -\tfrac{n}{2}\log 2\pi
$$ {#eq-lml}

The three terms have intuitive interpretations: the first penalises poor data fit,
the second penalises model complexity (larger $\ell$ = simpler model = smaller $|K|$),
and the third is a normalisation constant. Optimising @eq-lml over $\log\ell$ and
$\log\sigma^2$ with L-BFGS typically finds a good hyperparameter set in under 300
gradient evaluations.

```{julia}
#| label: fig-lml-surface
#| fig-cap: "Log marginal likelihood surface as a function of GP length scale ℓ and signal variance σ² on a 12-point sample from the wing-sweep range function. The optimum (star) is found automatically — no analyst tuning required."
#| code-fold: true

# Build LML surface for the wing-sweep training set
X_mat2  = reshape(x_c, 10, 1)
y_norm2 = (y_tr .- mean(y_tr)) ./ (std(y_tr) + 1e-8)

ℓ_grid  = range(0.05, 0.6, length=60)
σ²_grid = range(0.1, 5.0, length=60)

lml_surf = [log_marginal_likelihood(X_mat2, y_norm2; ℓ=ℓv, σ²=sv, σ_n=1e-3)
            for sv in σ²_grid, ℓv in ℓ_grid]
lml_surf = clamp.(lml_surf, maximum(lml_surf) - 20, Inf)

_, ker_opt, _ = let
    k, ym2, ys2 = optimise_gp_hyperparams(X_mat2, y_tr)
    (nothing, k, nothing)
end

contourf(ℓ_grid, σ²_grid, lml_surf, levels=15, color=:viridis,
         xlabel=L"Length scale $\ell$", ylabel=L"Signal variance $\sigma^2$",
         title="Log Marginal Likelihood")
scatter!([ker_opt.ℓ], [ker_opt.σ²], color=:gold, markersize=10,
         markershape=:star5, markerstrokecolor=:black, label="Optimum")
```

## Expected Improvement Acquisition

At each Bayesian optimisation step we must choose where to evaluate the black box
next. The **Expected Improvement** (EI) acquisition function provides a principled
answer: evaluate at the point that maximally improves on the current best, in
expectation over the GP posterior.

For **maximisation** with current best $f^* = \max_i y_i$:

$$
\text{EI}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(f(\mathbf{x}) - f^*, 0)\right]
= (m(\mathbf{x}) - f^* - \xi)\,\Phi(z) + \sigma(\mathbf{x})\,\phi(z)
$$ {#eq-ei}

$$
z = \frac{m(\mathbf{x}) - f^* - \xi}{\sigma(\mathbf{x})}
$$ {#eq-ei-z}

where $\Phi$ and $\phi$ are the standard normal CDF and PDF, and $\xi \geq 0$ is
an exploration bonus. When $\sigma(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ (at an observed point) EI is
zero — the algorithm never revisits the same point.

::: {.callout-note}
**EI vs. Nelder-Mead over RS.** BLISS-RS uses Nelder-Mead to minimise
$-\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})$ where $\hat{f}$ is the polynomial surface. This exploits
the current best estimate but ignores uncertainty: it will avoid high-uncertainty
regions even if they might contain the global optimum. EI naturally balances
exploitation (high $m$) against exploration (high $\sigma$).
:::

```{julia}
#| label: fig-ei-demo
#| fig-cap: "Expected Improvement acquisition after 8 observations on the wing-sweep slice. EI peaks in the gap between the known maximum (~55°) and the unexplored right flank — exactly the region where additional sampling would most improve the surrogate."
#| code-fold: true

f_best = maximum(y_tr)
y_n2   = (y_tr .- mean(y_tr)) ./ (std(y_tr) + 1e-8)
gp_n2  = fit_gp(reshape(x_c, 10, 1), y_n2, gp_n.kernel; σ_n=1e-3)
μ_n2, σ²_n2 = predict_gp(gp_n2, reshape(x_tc, 200, 1))
μ_sc   = μ_n2 .* std(y_tr) .+ mean(y_tr)
σ_sc   = sqrt.(σ²_n2) .* std(y_tr)
ei_vals = expected_improvement(μ_sc, σ_sc.^2, f_best; ξ=10.0)

p1 = plot(sweep_range, μ_sc, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="GP mean",
          ylabel="Range (NM)", xlabel="", title="GP Posterior",
          legend=:topleft)
plot!(sweep_range, μ_sc .- 2σ_sc, fillrange=μ_sc .+ 2σ_sc,
      fillalpha=0.18, color=:steelblue, lw=0, label="±2σ")
scatter!(x_tr, y_tr, color=:black, markersize=5, label="Obs.")
vline!([sweep_range[argmax(ei_vals)]], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash, label="Next EI")

p2 = plot(sweep_range, ei_vals, lw=2, color=:darkorange,
          fill=(0, 0.25, :darkorange), ylabel="EI", xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)",
          title="Expected Improvement", legend=false)
vline!([sweep_range[argmax(ei_vals)]], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash)

plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1), size=(680, 440))
```

## Latin Hypercube Sampling

BLISS-2026 replaces D-optimal designs with **Latin Hypercube Sampling** (LHS) for
the initial DOE. LHS divides each variable dimension into $n$ equal-probability
strata and places exactly one sample in each stratum, ensuring good marginal coverage
with no clustering.

```{julia}
#| label: fig-lhs-vs-dopt
#| fig-cap: "LHS (left) vs. D-optimal (right) for n=2 variables, 20 points. LHS distributes points more evenly across the interior; D-optimal concentrates them on the boundary where quadratic model accuracy is most critical. For GP surrogates — which interpolate rather than extrapolate — LHS is preferable."
#| code-fold: true

lhs_pts  = lhs_sample(2, 20; seed=42)
dopt_pts = d_optimal_points(2, 20; seed=42)

sk = (xlim=(-0.02,1.02), ylim=(-0.02,1.02), aspect_ratio=1,
      markersize=7, markerstrokecolor=:white, legend=false,
      grid=true, gridalpha=0.3, xlabel=L"x_1", ylabel=L"x_2")

p1 = scatter(lhs_pts[:,1],  lhs_pts[:,2];  color=:steelblue,
             title="LHS (20 pts)", sk...)
p2 = scatter(dopt_pts[:,1], dopt_pts[:,2]; color=:darkorange,
             title="D-Optimal (20 pts)", sk...)
plot(p1, p2, size=(660, 310))
```

## The BLISS-2026 Algorithm

The complete algorithm (implemented in `src/bliss_gp.jl`) is:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-bliss2026-flow
%%| fig-cap: "BLISS-2026 algorithm. The key difference from BLISS-RS (Fig. 10) is the inner Bayesian optimisation loop: after the initial LHS, additional points are placed adaptively under the Expected Improvement criterion rather than from a fixed DOE."
%%| fig-width: 4.8

flowchart TD
    A([Initialise Z bounds,\nset trust-region radius])
    B["Latin Hypercube Design\nn_lhs points in current bounds"]
    C["Parallel BB evaluation\nThreads.@threads"]
    D["Fit GP with auto hyperparams\nMaximise log p(y|X,θ)"]
    E["Bayesian optimisation inner loop\nAdd n_bo points via EI"]
    F["Best design Z* from GP\nwith uncertainty σ(Z*)"]
    G{Converged?\nouter iter = n_outer}
    H["Trust-region update\nShrink bounds around Z*"]
    I([Return Z*, obj*, GP posteriors])

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G -- No --> H --> B
    G -- Yes --> I

    classDef init fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1e3a5f
    classDef proc fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef gp   fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#14532d
    classDef dec  fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef done fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d

    class A init
    class B,C proc
    class D,E,F gp
    class G dec
    class H proc
    class I done
```

The formal statement of the BLISS-2026 system optimisation at each outer iteration
is:

$$
\text{Find:} \quad \mathbf{Z}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{Z} \in \mathcal{D}_k}
  \text{EI}(\mathbf{Z}; \hat{f}_{GP}, f^*_k, \xi)
$$ {#eq-bliss2026-obj}

where $\mathcal{D}_k$ is the trust-region at iteration $k$, $\hat{f}_{GP}$ is the
current GP posterior, and $f^*_k$ is the best observed objective so far. The
outer iterations progressively refine $\mathcal{D}_k$ via the trust-region update.

## Julia Implementation

```{julia}
#| label: lst-bliss2026-demo
#| code-fold: true

# Quick demonstration on the 2-variable Rosenbrock function
rosen2(Z) = -(100*(Z[2] - Z[1]^2)^2 + (Z[1]-1)^2)   # negated → maximise at (1,1)
bounds_demo = [-2.0 2.0; -1.0 3.0]

result = bliss_2026(rosen2, 2, bounds_demo;
                    n_lhs=12, n_bo=10, n_outer=5, K=0.7, verbose=true)
@printf("\nFinal Z* = [%.3f, %.3f],  obj* = %.4f  (true = 0.0)\n",
        result.Z_history[end]..., result.obj_history[end])
```

## BLISS-2026 on the Supersonic Business Jet

```{julia}
#| label: bliss2026-ssbj
#| code-fold: true

# System variable bounds: [t_c, ARw, Λw, Sw, SHT, ARHT, λ, h, M]
Z_lo_ssbj = [0.01, 3.5, 40.0, 200.0, 40.0, 3.5, 0.1, 35_000.0, 1.4]
Z_hi_ssbj = [0.10, 6.5, 70.0, 800.0, 125.0, 5.5, 0.4, 55_000.0, 2.2]
n_Z_ssbj  = 9

bounds_ssbj = hcat(Z_lo_ssbj, Z_hi_ssbj)

ssbj_bb(Z) = try
    r = ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0,
                      max_iter=20)
    max(r.Range, 0.0)
catch
    0.0
end

println("Running BLISS-2026 on supersonic business jet...")
result_gp = bliss_2026(ssbj_bb, n_Z_ssbj, bounds_ssbj;
                        n_lhs=25, n_bo=15, n_outer=8, K=0.7,
                        seed=42, verbose=true)
println("\nDone.")
```

## Convergence Comparison: BLISS-RS vs. BLISS-2026

```{julia}
#| label: fig-comparison-convergence
#| fig-cap: "Convergence comparison: BLISS-RS (quadratic RS + Nelder-Mead) vs. BLISS-2026 (GP + EI) on the supersonic business jet. BLISS-2026 reaches a higher objective value and shows smoother monotone improvement — the EI acquisition prevents wasted evaluations on already-explored regions."
#| code-fold: true

# Run BLISS-RS (from ch07 implementation)
using Optim

function run_blissrs_ssbj(n_iter=8, K=0.8; seed=42)
    bounds_ = copy(bounds_ssbj)
    Z_hist  = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    obj_hist = Float64[]
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)

    for it in 1:n_iter
        n_pts = max(min_rs_points(n_Z_ssbj) + 5, 55)
        pts_c = d_optimal_points(n_Z_ssbj, n_pts; seed=seed+it)
        pts_n = similar(pts_c)
        for j in 1:n_Z_ssbj
            lo, hi = bounds_[j,1], bounds_[j,2]
            pts_n[:,j] = lo .+ pts_c[:,j] .* (hi-lo)
        end
        y_vals = map(1:n_pts) do i
            try ssbj_bb(pts_n[i,:]) catch; 0.0 end
        end
        b = fit_rs(pts_c, y_vals)
        neg_rs(ξ) = -only(predict_rs(reshape(ξ,1,:), b))
        res = Optim.optimize(neg_rs, fill(0.5, n_Z_ssbj), NelderMead(),
                             Optim.Options(iterations=2000, show_trace=false))
        ξ_opt = clamp.(Optim.minimizer(res), 0.0, 1.0)
        Z_opt = [bounds_[j,1] + ξ_opt[j]*(bounds_[j,2]-bounds_[j,1]) for j in 1:n_Z_ssbj]
        obj   = try ssbj_bb(Z_opt) catch; 0.0 end
        push!(Z_hist, Z_opt)
        push!(obj_hist, obj)
        bounds_ = update_intervals(ξ_opt, bounds_; K=K)
    end
    return Z_hist, obj_hist
end

_, obj_rs = run_blissrs_ssbj(8, 0.8; seed=42)
obj_gp = result_gp.obj_history

p = plot(1:8, obj_rs, lw=2.5, color=:crimson, marker=:circle, markersize=6,
         label="BLISS-RS (quadratic RS + Nelder-Mead)",
         xlabel="Outer iteration", ylabel="Range (NM)",
         title="BLISS-RS vs. BLISS-2026: Supersonic Business Jet",
         legend=:bottomright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3)
plot!(1:8, obj_gp, lw=2.5, color=:steelblue, marker=:circle, markersize=6,
      label="BLISS-2026 (GP + EI)")
annotate!(8.15, obj_rs[end],   text(@sprintf("%.0f NM", obj_rs[end]),   9, :crimson, :left))
annotate!(8.15, obj_gp[end],   text(@sprintf("%.0f NM", obj_gp[end]),   9, :steelblue, :left))
```

```{julia}
#| label: fig-comparison-accuracy
#| fig-cap: "RS vs. GP surrogate accuracy comparison on 200 held-out ssbj range evaluations. Left: predicted vs. actual scatter — GP predictions lie closer to the diagonal. Right: normalised error distribution — GP is tighter with fewer outliers. Coverage shows the fraction of test points within ±2σ of the GP prediction."
#| code-fold: true

# Accuracy study: train on 40 LHS points, test on 200 random
comp = compare_rs_vs_gp(ssbj_bb, n_Z_ssbj, bounds_ssbj;
                         n_pts=40, n_test=200, seed=7)

p1 = scatter(comp.y_test, comp.y_rs, color=:crimson, alpha=0.5, markersize=4,
             label="BLISS-RS  RMSE=$(round(comp.rmse_rs,digits=0)) NM",
             xlabel="Actual range (NM)", ylabel="Predicted range (NM)",
             title="Predicted vs. Actual", legend=:topleft)
scatter!(comp.y_test, comp.y_gp, color=:steelblue, alpha=0.5, markersize=4,
         label="BLISS-2026  RMSE=$(round(comp.rmse_gp,digits=0)) NM")
xlims_ = extrema(comp.y_test); plot!([xlims_...],[xlims_...], color=:black, lw=1.5, label="")

err_rs = (comp.y_test .- comp.y_rs) ./ (abs.(comp.y_test) .+ 1e-6)
err_gp = (comp.y_test .- comp.y_gp) ./ (abs.(comp.y_test) .+ 1e-6)

p2 = stephist(clamp.(err_rs .* 100, -50, 50), bins=30, color=:crimson, alpha=0.55,
              label="BLISS-RS",
              xlabel="Normalised error (%)", ylabel="Count", title="Error Distribution")
stephist!(clamp.(err_gp .* 100, -50, 50), bins=30, color=:steelblue, alpha=0.55,
          label="BLISS-2026 (coverage=$(round(100*comp.coverage_gp,digits=0))%)")

plot(p1, p2, size=(780, 320))
```

## GP Uncertainty as a Design Tool

The GP posterior provides something the polynomial surrogate cannot: a calibrated
probability distribution over function values at any unsampled point. This has
immediate practical value in MDO:

1. **Stopping criterion**: When $\max_Z \sigma(Z) < \epsilon$ across the current
   search interval, the GP is confident everywhere — no further sampling is needed.
2. **Risk-aware optimisation**: Instead of maximising $\mu(\mathbf{Z})$, maximise
   $\mu(\mathbf{Z}) - k\sigma(\mathbf{Z})$ to find designs that are good *and*
   certain.
3. **Sensitivity analysis**: The GP length scales $\ell_i$ from an ARD kernel
   directly indicate variable importance — large $\ell_i$ means the objective
   is insensitive to variable $i$.

```{julia}
#| label: fig-gp-uncertainty-map
#| fig-cap: "GP predictive standard deviation over the wing sweep × Mach number subspace (all other variables at their optimal values from BLISS-2026). High uncertainty (bright) identifies where additional BB evaluations would most improve the surrogate. The star marks the BLISS-2026 optimum; the cross marks a high-uncertainty design the system should investigate before finalising."
#| code-fold: true

Z_opt_final = result_gp.Z_history[end]
gp_final    = result_gp.gp_models[end].gp

sweep_g = range(40.0, 70.0, length=50)
mach_g  = range(1.4, 2.2, length=50)

# Evaluate GP uncertainty over Λ_w × M subspace
# (use the GP trained in the last BLISS outer iteration)
# Build test grid in coded space
lo_sw2, hi_sw2 = bounds_ssbj[3,1], bounds_ssbj[3,2]
lo_M,   hi_M   = bounds_ssbj[9,1], bounds_ssbj[9,2]

sweep_c = (sweep_g .- lo_sw2) ./ (hi_sw2 - lo_sw2)
mach_c  = (mach_g  .- lo_M)  ./ (hi_M  - lo_M)

# For 2D slice, code all other dims at their optimum
Z_opt_c = to_coded(Z_opt_final, bounds_ssbj)
σ_grid  = [begin
    x_test = copy(Z_opt_c)
    x_test[3] = sc; x_test[9] = mc
    _, σ²v = predict_gp(gp_final, reshape(x_test, 1, :))
    sqrt(max(σ²v[1], 0.0)) * result_gp.gp_models[end].y_std
end for mc in mach_c, sc in sweep_c]

heatmap(sweep_g, mach_g, σ_grid,
        xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)", ylabel="Mach number M",
        title="GP Predictive Std. Dev. σ(Z) — Range (NM)",
        color=:hot, colorbar_title="σ (NM)")
scatter!([Z_opt_final[3]], [Z_opt_final[9]], color=:cyan, markersize=10,
         markershape=:star5, markerstrokecolor=:black, label="BLISS-2026 optimum")
i_hi = argmax(σ_grid)
scatter!([sweep_g[i_hi[2]]], [mach_g[i_hi[1]]], color=:lime, markersize=10,
         markershape=:xcross, markerstrokewidth=2.5, label="High-σ candidate")
```

## Summary: BLISS-RS vs. BLISS-2026

```{julia}
#| label: tbl-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "Feature-by-feature comparison of BLISS-RS (Altus 2002) and BLISS-2026."

rows = [
    ("Surrogate type",            "Quadratic polynomial",         "Gaussian Process (SE kernel)"),
    ("Surrogate fit",             "Least squares (analytic)",     "Cholesky inference, O(n³)"),
    ("Hyperparameters",           "None (fixed structure)",        "ℓ, σ² via marginal likelihood"),
    ("Uncertainty estimates",     "None",                          "Full predictive distribution"),
    ("Initial DOE",               "D-Optimal",                    "Latin Hypercube Sampling"),
    ("Adaptive sampling",         "None (fixed budget per iter)",  "Expected Improvement (EI)"),
    ("System optimiser",          "Nelder-Mead over polynomial",  "Bayesian optim. (max EI)"),
    ("Parallel BB eval",          "Conceptual only",              "Threads.@threads"),
    ("Bounds management",         "Fixed 20% shrinkage",          "Trust-region update"),
    ("Extrapolation quality",     "Polynomial (can oscillate)",   "Reverts to GP prior"),
    ("Preferred for",             "Low-dim, smooth, fast BBs",    "Any dim, noisy/expensive BBs"),
]

println("| Property | BLISS-RS (2002) | BLISS-2026 |")
println("|----------|----------------|-----------|")
for (p, rs, gp) in rows
    println("| $p | $rs | $gp |")
end
```
